reasoning: Recent developments in late Oct/early Nov 2025 have elevated the baseline risk of a nuclear test: Trump ordered the US to resume testing, prompting Putin to order his ministers to draft proposals for a Russian nuclear test in response. Furthermore, Russia's Defense Minister has claimed the Arctic Novaya Zemlya site is 'ready at short notice,' and independent satellite imagery confirms ongoing tunnel work. Despite these alarming signals, a test before January 1, 2026 remains unlikely. First, Putin has consistently framed any potential test as a 'reciprocal measure' dependent on the US acting first. Nuclear experts estimate it will take the US at least 36 months to physically restart tests in Nevada. Second, while a bare-bones 'political' demonstration test could theoretically be organized quickly, a fully instrumented scientific test takes approximately 6 months to prepare. With less than 8 weeks remaining in 2025, the logistical and political realities push against a test occurring before the end of the year unless Russia radically abbreviates its timeline and abandons its stated policy of reciprocity. The probability is significantly higher than the 0.05 market baseline from late October, but still firmly on the low side for 2025.
